text: This is the intraoperative picture with a large cystic mass with smooth borders, no ascites, no significantly increased vascularity of the mass.

While retracting bowel with laparoscopic shearer at this particular point there was injury to serosa of the sigmoid colon which we did not recognize until later as it was really unexpected to happen with the mere touching of the bowel with cold instrument like this.
We move to right side to release the omental adhesion from the right pelvic wall.
Then we started to identify left IP ligament.
For this congenital bowel adhesions were released along the white line of Toll.
Gradually IP ligament is skeletonized.
Left ureter was seen transperitoneally and IP ligament was coagulated
staying safely away from the ureter.
Then incidental finding of sigmoid colon injury was noticed, but we did not get panic and continued our surgery.
Next step was to create the window in the peritoneum to isolate the IP ligament completely from the ureter and other structures.
After this, we intended to perform complete ureterolysis on the left side.
But you can see the extent of the mass reaching into the deep pelvis.
Also, the retroperitoneum is little fibrosed and the peritoneum is not clearly separable from underlying ureter.

even with the moderate force by Shearer this could be due to the secondary to previous surgery which led to inflammatory changes in the retroperitoneum so we shifted to the other boundary of the dissection and that is lateral one peritoneum here also is really thick and not as pliable as seen normally look at the extent of the mass it is
almost reaching till the lateral pelvic wall.
Taking some bold cuts could lead to some separation of the mass from lateral pelvic wall.
In continuation with this, round ligament is cut and some areolar tissue is witnessed finally.
Once lateral border is made free from attachments, the IP ligament is finally cut so that this mass can be reflected medially and proper exposure can be achieved.
This led to access to POD.
We found that small bowel is densely adherent to the posterior surface of the mass.
Then the attachments of the base are released by small cuts by laparoscopic shearer.
This mass was also found to be adherent to the dome of the bladder and the apex of the vault on the left side.
We started working on this border by sharp dissection.
and layer by layer adhesions were liberated.
The bowel could not be taken out of the pelvis completely because of the dense adhesions to the mass.
Ideally, complete ureterolysis should have been done, but because of the fibrous nature of retroperitoneum, it couldn't be done.
Fortunately, we could locate the ureter transperitoneally all the time.
That gave us confidence to cut the posterior peritoneal fold.
Then, boil adhesions were liberated.
To begin with, we started with the suction cannula, but we then shifted to laparoscopic shearer to
cut the few adhesions.
And finally we had to take the scissors to be on the safer side to avoid the thermal injury to go away.
Meanwhile, the cyst got punctured unknowingly
to deflate it completely and have a good working space in the pelvis.
For concluding oophorectomy, last few cuts were taken and then we thought we are through.
But within few seconds, we realized we have yet another bowel serosal injury.
Maybe a good traction and a telescopic visualization from all around the adhesion point could have prevented this.
So now we have bowel injury at two places to deal with.
But the good part is it is a serosal injury only and the area of injury is pretty small.
Finishing oophorectomy.
with the last cuts and the specimen is delivered out through a glow bag through the side port.
Now the serosal injury is repaired with the Vicryl No.1 taking superficial bite through the serosa.
avoiding going through the mucosa.
2 to 3 intermittent reinforcing stitches were taken.
In our limited experience of such serosal injury, we prefer suturing the serosal injury rather than leaving it.
In this particular patient, drain was kept
and she was kept NBM for three days.
She had an uneventful recovery in post-operative period.
Similarly, sigmoid colon injury was sutured, taking the suture from the right side of the patient.
